summary: Hungary faces rising special educational needs (SEN/SNI/BTMN) identification amid demographic contraction, and six transformation paths define the option space without a single winner. TP-live-t1, a passive baseline, changes nothing in gatekeeping, categories, or finance, serving mainly as an analytical counterfactual. Empirically, cohorts are already shrinking [F-live-demography-01][F-live-demography-02] while the specialist workforce ages and concentrates unevenly [F-live-demography-06], and legal exposure (ECtHR, EU infringement) remains open [F-live-legal_and_governance-09][F-live-political_feasibility-06][F-live-political_feasibility-09]. Multiple lenses argue inaction is not neutral since sorting and strain compound [F-live-hungarian_education_system-02][F-live-school_network_planning-05]; this is a value choice about whether deliberate non-intervention is acceptable, not merely an empirical question.　TP-live-t2 builds a reconciled measurement architecture to separate true prevalence from diagnostic-practice, definitional, and incentive effects, using divergent series [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01][F-live-education_finance-01] and rule-change identification points like the 2020 readiness reform [F-live-hungarian_education_system-08][F-live-school_network_planning-04]. Comparative evidence (OECD autism detection effects [F-live-demography-05][F-live-international_comparison-03], Finland's tier-concentration pattern [F-live-finnish_reform-02]) supports the decomposition logic strongly, though data alone cannot compel political action on findings.　TP-live-t3 restructures categories and the sole nationwide expert-committee gatekeeper [F-live-hungarian_education_system-04], addressing documented waiting lists [F-live-implementation_planning-03] via non-categorical models like Wales' ALN [F-live-international_comparison-06] and Finland's tiers [F-live-international_comparison-07]. Constraints are serious: Finland retains 4%-13% municipal variation post-reform [F-live-finnish_reform-04], only 23% of top-tier pupils are fully included [F-live-finnish_reform-05], and Poland's rapid, non-consensual 2016-2017 restructuring caused a placement crisis and teacher strikes [F-live-polish_reform-06][F-live-polish_reform-07][F-live-polish_reform-09]. Decision-makers must choose speed and consultation depth against disruption risk.　TP-live-t4 shifts finance from diagnosis-gated multipliers [F-live-education_finance-08][F-live-legal_and_governance-07] toward census-based allocation, following Finland's coincidental identification-reduction pattern [F-live-finnish_reform-03] and England's funding-linked EHCP disparities [F-live-international_comparison-04][F-live-international_comparison-05]. This rests on an unproven causal theory for Hungary specifically, and Poland's contrasting expansion of disability-weighted funding independent of prevalence [F-live-polish_reform-03][F-live-polish_reform-04] weakens claims of universal convergence—an empirical gap requiring targeted testing rather than a value judgment.　TP-live-t5 expands specialist workforce with regional targeting, directly matching documented shortages (Budapest concentration versus rural scarcity [F-live-hungarian_education_system-05][F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05], northeastern gaps [F-live-school_network_planning-05], and the assessment-to-therapy shortfall [F-live-implementation_planning-04][F-live-implementation_planning-05]). It is broadly low-conflict politically [F-live-political_feasibility-05] but constrained by uncertain training-to-practice conversion [F-live-education_finance-07] and untargeted salary reforms [F-live-portuguese_reform-03].　TP-live-t6 coordinates anti-segregation safeguards across free school choice, selective tracks, readiness rules, and private/church growth, responding to unresolved ECtHR/EU obligations [F-live-legal_and_governance-09][F-live-legal_and_governance-10] and documented choice-driven sorting [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08][F-live-hungarian_education_system-09]. This has the strongest problem-diagnosis grounding but faces the deepest institutional resistance, given a decade of unresolved compliance [F-live-political_feasibility-06][F-live-political_feasibility-08] and maintainer-autonomy questions extending into private and church sectors.　Across all paths, decision-makers must weigh: whether transparency (t2) is a precondition or substitute for structural change; whether disruption risk from rapid restructuring (t3) outweighs bottleneck relief; whether unproven finance theory (t4) justifies systemic redesign; whether capacity expansion alone (t5) suffices without entitlement reform; and whether anti-segregation coordination (t6) is achievable without external enforcement. The research most likely to change these choices concerns the causal decomposition of identification drivers, true regional need versus administrative capacity, and whether Hungary's specific financing and gatekeeping mechanisms causally drive over-identification—open empirical questions distinct from the values debate over acceptable risk, pace, and institutional autonomy trade-offs embedded in each path.
